title: Stabilizing the parquet problem
authors: Herbert Eßl, Stefan Rohshap, Marcel Gievers, Markus Wallerberger, Alessandro Toschi, Anna Kauch
abstract: We systematically analyze the stability of the iterative solution of the parquet equations by studying the spectrum of the Jacobian associated with the commonly used damped fixed-point iteration procedure. In this context, we provide an explicit criterion that determines when the physical fixed point of the parquet iteration becomes unstable. Importantly, we demonstrate that misleading convergence issues, observed in parquet calculation at intermediate-to-high interaction values, are not restricted to parameter regions where the two-particle irreducible vertex diverges, but can also arise in absence of vertex divergences. Hence, the misleading convergence issues of parquet-based algorithms are not directly caused by the crossings of two solutions of the (multivalued) Luttinger-Ward functional, that are associated with vertex divergences. Building on these insights, we introduce a controlled stabilization strategy that allows the convergence to the physical solution in the instability regimes. We apply this procedure to the zero-point model and the Hubbard model in the atomic limit, where we successfully stabilize the physical solution deep in the non-perturbative regime, even across multiple divergence lines.
summary: This work addresses the numerical instability of the parquet equations used to calculate properties of strongly correlated electron systems like the Hubbard model. By analyzing the Jacobian spectrum, the authors provide a stabilization strategy that allows the iterative solution to converge to the correct physical fixed point even in highly non-perturbative regimes where standard methods fail.
main_problem: The paper systematically analyzes the stability of the iterative solution of the parquet equations, which are used to solve for interacting many-body systems.
main_result: The instability of the parquet iteration is not solely caused by divergences in the two-particle irreducible vertex, and a controlled stabilization strategy was introduced that successfully converges to the physical solution in deep non-perturbative regimes.
method: The analysis involves studying the spectrum of the Jacobian associated with the damped fixed-point iteration of the parquet equations, leading to a stabilization procedure.
model_or_system: The study applies to the Zero-point (ZP) model and the Hubbard atom (HA), which represents the strong-coupling limit of the Hubbard model. The formalism relies on diagrammatic techniques like the parquet equations.
key_observables: Convergence issues, Jacobian eigenvalues, physical fixed point stability, and the two-particle irreducible vertex.
important_parameters: Interaction strength (U), chemical potential deviation ($\delta\mu$), and the damping parameter ($p$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the validity of the iterative fixed-point approach, though it shows this approach can fail due to local instabilities.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the stability phase space for the ZP model, comparing conventional iteration failure versus success using the stabilized method, and show convergence trajectories for coupled equations.
paper_structure: The paper proceeds by analyzing the stability of the iterative solution, deriving stability criteria from the Jacobian spectrum, introducing a stabilization strategy, and finally applying this procedure to the ZP and HA models.
why_interesting: The rigorous mathematical treatment of convergence failure in diagrammatic methods provides crucial insight into the non-perturbative physics of strongly correlated materials, which is central to modern condensed matter theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The eigenvalues λ (black circles) for the parquet formalism of the HA for two different values of U at ph-symmetry in the complex plane. Colorful circles show the stability regions for given damping parameters p. Left: Fixed point can be stabilized with damping, i.e., by decreasing p. Right: Fixed point cannot be stabilized with damping since some eigenvalues have a negative real part. The vertical black line marks where Re(λ) = 0.

Figure 2: Trajectories of fixed-point iterations with the iterative map f = x+µx− x2, Eq. (18), for different starting points x0 ∈[−3.49, 3.51] in steps of ∆x0 = 0.1. Black (red) dashed lines indicate the (un)physical fixed point x∗= 0(µ). The first column of panels shows the fixed-point iteration for the equation µ = −1, the middle column for µ = −3, and the third one for µ = 1. Panels (a)–(c) are computed with a damping parameter p = 1.0, while panels (d)–(f) with p = 0.2, which fixes the convergence problems of the case µ = −3. In panel (i), we apply the stabilization method by switching the sign of the damping parameter (p = −1.0) resulting in convergence to the physical fixed point....

Figure 3: Trajectories of the fixed-point iteration for the map fx = 1 + 2x + ηy and fy = 1 −2y + ηx with η = 0.1 for different starting points (x0, y0) with x0, y0 ∈(−5, 5) and 40 iterations. In panel (a), the damping p = 1.0 leads to unstable directions along both eigenvectors u1 = (0.0250, −0.9997)T , u2 = (−0.9997, −0.0250)T . In panel (b), the damping p is set to 0.2 stabilizing the direction along u1 (associated with λ1 = −2.0025). Application of the stabilization method in panel (c) fixes the stability issues and allows for convergence of the fixed-point iteration to the correct solution (x∗, y∗) = (−1.03, 0.299) (red dot).

Figure 4: Stability phase space of the parquet formalism with damped iteration, Eq. (10), for the ZP model. Color scheme: In the green region, the fixed point is stable without damping (p = 1); in the orange region, it is unstable for p = 1 but curable by smaller p; in the red region, it is unstable and not curable by a smaller p. The vertex divergence, which only appears in the density channel at (Re(δµ) = 0, U = Im(δµ)2), is marked by a black square.

Figure 5: Parquet results for the ZP model with conventional iteration (orange ◦ denoted by p) and stabilized iteration (blue + denoted by P) for ph-symmetry (Re(δµ) = 0), where the red lines mark the instabilities (solid for vertex divergences and dashed for real eigenvalues that go through zero). The physical solution is shown as gray solid line. The starting point for each calculation is the physical fixed point at the previous U value and the damping p is determined by Eq. (13) with c = 0.1. In panels (a)–(e), the channels (d,m,G,s,t) are shown and in panel (f) the real part of eigenvalues of Π are shown, where purely real eigenvalues are marked by magenta + and complex eigenvalues are...
